The Junior Engineer Made the Right Call. I Overruled Him. Production Proved Him Right Six Hours Later.

7 min read
Just now

--

The Junior Engineer Made the Right Call. I Overruled Him. Production Proved Him Right Six Hours Later.
I had more experience. That was the problem.
The Code Review That Became a Lesson
It was a Thursday afternoon and I was reviewing a PR that one of our junior engineers had submitted for the rate limiting implementation on our public API. Tariq had been with us five months. He was careful, methodical, asked good questions in design discussions. His code was clean in the way that junior engineers who care about their craft produce clean code.
The PR was implementing a token bucket algorithm for rate limiting at the API gateway level. Straightforward implementation, well-tested, readable. The test coverage was good.
There was one part I disagreed with. Tariq had implemented the rate limit state in Redis with a TTL that reset on every request rather than expiring at the end of the rate window. I had seen this pattern before and I had seen it cause problems. I left a comment explaining that the correct approach was a fixed window implementation with a TTL that expired at the end of the window rather than rolling with each request. I marked it as a required change before merge.
Tariq responded to the comment. He said he had considered the fixed window approach and had deliberately chosen the rolling TTL because our API had a burst traffic pattern during the first thirty seconds of each minute due to scheduled jobs running on the hour, and the fixed window would create a thundering herd problem at the window boundary when all the scheduled jobs reset simultaneously and found their counters at zero.
I read his response. I understood the reasoning. I overruled him anyway.
I said the rolling TTL introduced complexity that the fixed window did not have and that the thundering herd concern was theoretical and could be addressed if it materialized. I approved the PR with the fixed window implementation.
The Six Hours That Followed the Deploy
We deployed on Thursday evening. The scheduled jobs ran at the top of the hour as they always did. At 9:00 PM our API error rate spiked to thirty-one percent and stayed there for four minutes while every scheduled job that had been rate limited in the previous window simultaneously found its counter reset and retried its backlog of queued requests against a gateway that was now seeing three times normal traffic in a forty-five second window.
The thundering herd that Tariq had identified as theoretical was not theoretical. It was a four-minute error spike that affected eleven downstream systems and generated forty-seven customer-facing errors that the support team fielded over the following two hours.
I rolled back to the rolling TTL implementation at 9:07 PM. The spike stopped. The scheduled jobs processed normally against a rate limiter that was designed for their actual traffic pattern rather than for a traffic pattern that was easier to reason about in a code review.
What I Had Actually Overruled
After the rollback I sat with what had happened long enough to understand it correctly.
Tariq had not made a junior mistake. He had made a senior observation. He had looked at the actual traffic pattern of the system he was building for, identified a specific failure mode that the simpler implementation would produce under that pattern, and built a solution that handled the failure mode at the cost of some additional complexity.
I had looked at the implementation and pattern-matched it against a category of problem I had seen before, which was rolling TTL implementations that were chosen for the wrong reasons and produced subtle correctness issues. That pattern match was valid in general. It was wrong in this specific context because the specific context had a traffic characteristic that made the fixed window the worse choice.
My experience had not made me more right. It had made me faster to reach a conclusion that my pattern library supported without examining whether the pattern applied. Tariq had not had the pattern library to draw on and had been forced to reason from the actual system behavior. The reasoning from actual system behavior had been more accurate than the reasoning from accumulated patterns.
That is not an argument against experience. Experience produces pattern libraries that are right far more often than reasoning from first principles in every individual case. But experience also produces the specific failure mode of applying the right pattern to the wrong context, and the confidence that comes with experience makes that failure mode harder to catch because the confidence suppresses the question of whether the pattern fits.
What the Review Comment Should Have Said
When Tariq responded to my comment explaining his reasoning, I had two pieces of information I had not had before. I had the implementation he had chosen and I had the reasoning behind it. The reasoning was specific and grounded in the actual traffic behavior of the system.
The review comment I left after reading his response should have been: walk me through the burst pattern you identified and show me the data that supports it. Not because I doubted the reasoning but because that question would have forced both of us to examine whether the pattern was as consistent as his mental model of it suggested, and whether the rolling TTL fully addressed the burst without introducing its own edge cases.
Instead I said the concern was theoretical and overruled the implementation.
Theoretical is a word that experienced engineers use when they have not done the work of testing whether the theory is grounded. Tariq had done that work. He had looked at the actual traffic distribution across our scheduled job fleet and identified a real pattern. I had called it theoretical because I had not looked at the data myself and because my pattern library said fixed window was the right answer and I had not questioned whether my pattern library was right for this context.
What I Changed About How I Review Junior Code
The practice I changed after that Thursday is simple and uncomfortable in the specific way that admitting you were wrong to a junior engineer is uncomfortable when you have been in the industry for longer than they have been writing code professionally.
I went to Tariq the following morning and told him directly that his implementation had been correct and my override had been wrong and that the reasoning he had presented in the review comment had been sound and I had not examined it carefully enough before overruling it.
He was gracious about it in the way that junior engineers are gracious when a senior engineer admits a mistake, which is carefully and with visible relief that the admission was made.
What I changed in how I run reviews is the question I ask when a junior engineer pushes back on a comment I have left. The question is not whether their reasoning is more sophisticated than I expected. The question is whether their reasoning is grounded in something about this specific system that my pattern match did not account for. Those are different questions. The first one is about the engineer. The second one is about the context. The context is usually where the important information lives.
Junior engineers who push back on review comments are not always right. They are sometimes right in the specific way that Tariq was right, which is that they are closer to the actual system behavior because they built the thing and reasoned from what they observed rather than from what the pattern library suggested. That proximity is not a substitute for experience but it is occasionally more accurate than experience when experience produces pattern application rather than contextual reasoning.
The Confidence That Blocks the Question
The failure mode I exhibited in that review is available at every level of seniority and becomes more dangerous as seniority increases. Junior engineers second-guess their pattern matches because they do not have enough of them to trust. Senior engineers stop second-guessing their pattern matches because the patterns have been right often enough that the second-guessing feels unnecessary.
The patterns are right often enough that the second-guessing is unnecessary most of the time. The times it is necessary are the times when the context has a characteristic the pattern does not account for and the confidence that comes with experience suppresses the question that would surface the characteristic before the deploy rather than six hours after it.
Tariq had surfaced the characteristic in the review comment. I had read it and overruled it because my pattern said fixed window and his reasoning was junior and the confidence that comes with experience is sometimes indistinguishable from the confidence that comes from not having examined whether the pattern fits.
Six hours later production examined it for me.
The Judgment That Knows When to Question the Pattern
The diagnostic habits that distinguish pattern application from contextual reasoning, the code review practices that surface what junior engineers know about the specific system that the reviewer’s pattern library does not account for, the failure modes that experience produces and that seniority makes harder to catch, and the traffic pattern analysis that turns theoretical concerns into grounded ones before the deploy rather than after it that is inside The Senior Backend Engineer Handbook.
Because the most expensive overrides are not the ones where the junior engineer was wrong. They are the ones where the junior engineer had looked at the actual system and the senior engineer had looked at the pattern library and the pattern library was right in general and wrong in this specific context and production was the thing that resolved the disagreement.
Tariq was right. I had more experience. Production sorted it out in six hours.
The longer code review and incident breakdowns go out on Substack.
